sections.title: Virtualization - CPU
sections.subtitle: How the OS creates the illusion that each process has its own CPU

lessons.title: What is a Process?
lessons.objective: Understand process state, PCB fields, and state transitions.

concepts: A process is a running program managed by the OS.
Process state includes memory, registers, program counter, and open files.
Process state machine: running, ready, blocked with deterministic transitions.
The PCB stores scheduler-visible and kernel-visible process metadata.
Running means currently on CPU, Ready means runnable but waiting, Blocked means waiting for an event.
State transitions are triggered by events such as dispatch, I/O block, wakeup, and exit.

challenge.description: Control three processes and inspect state transitions tick-by-tick.

lessons.title: Process API - fork, exec, wait
lessons.objective: Use fork/exec/wait correctly and identify zombie process outcomes.

concepts: fork creates a child process that continues from the same point.
exec replaces a process image with a new program.
wait blocks parent until child completion.
Skipping wait can create zombies until parent reaps child status.
Parent and child are distinguished by fork return value.
fork plus exec is a core shell pattern that separates process creation from program replacement.

challenge.description: Act as a shell and execute commands in sequence with correct parent-child synchronization.

lessons.title: Limited Direct Execution
lessons.objective: Trace syscall trap flow and timer interrupt context-switch behavior.

concepts: User mode and kernel mode separate privilege levels.
System calls trap from user to kernel via trap table entries.
Timer interrupts return control to OS for preemption.
Context switch saves/restores register state between processes.
Limited direct execution keeps programs fast while preserving OS control.
Trap entry and return must preserve correctness of user-visible execution state.

challenge.description: Complete syscall round-trip and forced preemption scenarios.

parts.title: System Call Round Trip
parts.objective: Walk user trap to kernel handler and back to user mode.
parts.description: Manually execute trap lifecycle for file-read syscall.

parts.title: Timer Interrupt and Context Switch
parts.objective: Observe forced preemption and process switch ordering.
parts.description: Use timer interrupts to trigger scheduler decisions.

lessons.title: CPU Scheduling Basics (FIFO, SJF, STCF)
lessons.objective: Compare policy impact on turnaround, response, and fairness.

concepts: FIFO is simple but suffers convoy effects.
SJF minimizes turnaround when durations are known and arrivals are aligned.
STCF preempts to approximate shortest remaining work.
Policy quality depends on workload arrival and burst patterns.
Turnaround, response, and fairness move differently under each policy.
Side-by-side comparisons reveal why no single policy dominates all workloads.

challenge.description: Configure arrivals and bursts, then compare three baseline policies.

lessons.title: Round Robin
lessons.objective: Tune quantum to balance response time and throughput.

concepts: Round Robin cycles runnable jobs using fixed quanta.
Smaller quantum improves response but increases context switch overhead.
Larger quantum improves throughput but can hurt interactivity.
Context switch cost shapes practical scheduling tradeoffs.
Quantum tuning is a balance between latency for interactive work and total completion efficiency.
You cannot optimize response and turnaround simultaneously for all workloads.

challenge.description: Adjust quantum under interactive load and analyze tradeoff curves.

lessons.title: Multi-Level Feedback Queue (MLFQ)
lessons.objective: Understand demotion, boosting, and anti-gaming controls.

concepts: MLFQ approximates SJF by adapting to observed CPU usage.
CPU-bound jobs are demoted after consuming full quantum.
Priority boosts prevent starvation.
Anti-gaming rules track cumulative CPU usage, not only per-quantum behavior.
I/O-bound jobs often remain in higher-priority queues due to short CPU bursts.
Boost intervals and demotion rules determine long-run fairness and responsiveness.

challenge.description: Visualize queue movement and detect scheduler gaming behavior.

parts.title: Basic MLFQ
parts.objective: Track demotion path of long-running CPU-bound jobs.
parts.description: Observe movement from top queue to lower queues over time.

parts.title: Catch the Gamer
parts.objective: Detect and mitigate near-quantum relinquish gaming.
parts.description: Toggle anti-gaming rules and compare fairness outcomes.

lessons.title: Lottery and Stride Scheduling
lessons.objective: Allocate CPU proportionally and compare probabilistic vs deterministic sharing.

concepts: Lottery uses ticket draws for proportional scheduling.
Stride provides deterministic proportional share with pass values.
Ticket transfer and inflation alter effective priorities.
Long-run share convergence can be measured against target percentages.
Lottery gives probabilistic fairness while stride gives deterministic selection order.
Proportional-share scheduling is useful when service-level CPU ratios matter.

challenge.description: Configure target shares 50/30/20 and validate observed CPU allocation.

lessons.title: Multi-CPU Scheduling
lessons.objective: Balance two CPUs while preserving affinity and reducing imbalance.

concepts: Single-queue multiprocessor scheduling can hurt affinity and scalability.
Multi-queue scheduling improves locality but can become imbalanced.
Migration and work-stealing address idle CPU scenarios.
Cache warmth and migration cost should be visible in policy decisions.
Load balancing must trade utilization gains against cache-locality losses.
Affinity-aware migration policy is essential for realistic multi-CPU performance.

challenge.description: Operate two CPU queues, migrate jobs, and tune stealing/affinity behavior.
